\medskip
\noindent\textit{Summary:} The isomorphism $D_2 \cong \text{Bool}$ is now proven in both 
directions. Distinction and truth are the same structure.

%% ============================================================
\chapter{The Graph Emerges}
\label{chap:graph-emerges}
%% ============================================================

The preceding chapters have established our mathematical toolkit: distinction, logic, 
numbers, rationals, and reals. All emerged by logical necessity from $D_0$.

Now comes the central construction. We show that a specific graph—the complete graph on 
four vertices, $K_4$—emerges inevitably from the structure of distinction. This is not a 
choice; it is forced. The graph $K_4$ will be the geometric core from which all physics 
derives.

\section{Formalizing Unavoidability}

We proved earlier that distinction cannot be denied without being invoked 
(see \texttt{distinction-unavoidable}). Here we generalize this to a record 
type that captures unavoidability for any proposition $P$: both asserting 
and denying $P$ require the ability to distinguish.

\begin{code}%
\>[0]\AgdaKeyword{record}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaRecord{Unavoidable}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaBound{P}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaPrimitive{Set}\AgdaSymbol{)}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaPrimitive{Set}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaKeyword{where}\<%
\\
\>[0][@{}l@{\AgdaIndent{0}}]%
\>[2]\AgdaKeyword{field}\<%
\\
\>[2][@{}l@{\AgdaIndent{0}}]%
\>[4]\AgdaField{assertion-uses-D₀}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{P}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{Distinction}\<%
\\
%
\>[4]\AgdaField{denial-uses-D₀}%
\>[22]\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaFunction{¬}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{P}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{Distinction}\<%
\\
%
\\[\AgdaEmptyExtraSkip]%
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{unavoidability-of-D₀}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaRecord{Unavoidable}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{Distinction}\<%
\\
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{unavoidability-of-D₀}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaKeyword{record}\<%
\\
\>[0][@{}l@{\AgdaIndent{0}}]%
\>[2]\AgdaSymbol{\{}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{assertion-uses-D₀}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{λ}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{d}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{d}\<%
\\
%
\>[2]\AgdaSymbol{;}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{denial-uses-D₀}%
\>[22]\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{λ}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{\AgdaUnderscore{}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{φ}\<%
\\
%
\>[2]\AgdaSymbol{\}}\<%
\end{code}

This record will be used throughout the derivation to verify that each step 
traces back to the unavoidable First Distinction.

\section{One-Point Compactification}

A crucial construction for connecting the discrete and continuous is 
\emph{one-point compactification}. Given any set $A$, we add a single 
point $\infty$ representing "infinity" or "the boundary at the edge of 
the world."

\begin{code}%
\>[0]\AgdaKeyword{data}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaDatatype{OnePointCompactification}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaBound{A}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaPrimitive{Set}\AgdaSymbol{)}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaPrimitive{Set}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaKeyword{where}\<%
\\
\>[0][@{}l@{\AgdaIndent{0}}]%
\>[2]\AgdaInductiveConstructor{embed}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{A}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaDatatype{OnePointCompactification}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{A}\<%
\\
%
\>[2]\AgdaInductiveConstructor{∞}%
\>[8]\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaDatatype{OnePointCompactification}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{A}\<%
\\
\>[0]\<%
\end{code}

\subsection{The Universal Property: Why +1 is Forced}

The one-point compactification is not arbitrary. It is the \textbf{free pointed set} 
over $A$---the canonical way to add a distinguished basepoint to any set. This is a 
\emph{universal construction} in category theory.

\paragraph{Pointed Sets.}
A \emph{pointed set} is a pair $(X, x_0)$ where $X$ is a set and $x_0 \in X$ is a 
distinguished element (the basepoint). A morphism of pointed sets preserves the basepoint.

\begin{code}%
\>[0]\AgdaKeyword{record}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaRecord{PointedSet}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaPrimitive{Set₁}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaKeyword{where}\<%
\\
\>[0][@{}l@{\AgdaIndent{0}}]%
\>[2]\AgdaKeyword{field}\<%
\\
\>[2][@{}l@{\AgdaIndent{0}}]%
\>[4]\AgdaField{carrier}%
\>[14]\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaPrimitive{Set}\<%
\\
%
\>[4]\AgdaField{basepoint}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{carrier}\<%
\\
%
\\[\AgdaEmptyExtraSkip]%
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{makePointed}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaBound{A}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaPrimitive{Set}\AgdaSymbol{)}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaRecord{PointedSet}\<%
\\
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{makePointed}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{A}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaKeyword{record}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{\{}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{carrier}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaDatatype{OnePointCompactification}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{A}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{;}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{basepoint}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{∞}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{\}}\<%
\end{code}

\paragraph{The Universal Property.}
For any set $A$ and any pointed set $(Y, y_0)$, there is a \emph{unique} way to extend 
a function $f : A \to Y$ to a pointed map $\bar{f} : A_+ \to Y$ (where $A_+ = A \sqcup \{*\}$).
This is the defining property of the free pointed set:

\[
\text{Hom}_{\mathbf{Set}_*}(A_+, (Y, y_0)) \cong \text{Hom}_{\mathbf{Set}}(A, Y)
\]

The "+1" is not chosen---it is \emph{forced} by this universal property.

\begin{code}%
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{extend-to-pointed}%
\>[16729I]\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{\{}\AgdaBound{A}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaPrimitive{Set}\AgdaSymbol{\}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{\{}\AgdaBound{Y}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaPrimitive{Set}\AgdaSymbol{\}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaBound{f}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{A}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{Y}\AgdaSymbol{)}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaBound{y₀}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{Y}\AgdaSymbol{)}\<%
\\
\>[.][@{}l@{}]\<[16729I]%
\>[18]\AgdaSymbol{→}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaDatatype{OnePointCompactification}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{A}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{Y}\AgdaSymbol{)}\<%
\\
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{extend-to-pointed}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{f}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{y₀}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaInductiveConstructor{embed}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{a}\AgdaSymbol{)}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{f}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{a}\<%
\\
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{extend-to-pointed}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{f}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{y₀}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{∞}%
\>[33]\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{y₀}\<%
\\
%
\\[\AgdaEmptyExtraSkip]%
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{extend-preserves-basepoint}%
\>[16761I]\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{\{}\AgdaBound{A}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaPrimitive{Set}\AgdaSymbol{\}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{\{}\AgdaBound{Y}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaPrimitive{Set}\AgdaSymbol{\}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaBound{f}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{A}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{Y}\AgdaSymbol{)}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaBound{y₀}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{Y}\AgdaSymbol{)}\<%
\\
\>[.][@{}l@{}]\<[16761I]%
\>[27]\AgdaSymbol{→}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{extend-to-pointed}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{f}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{y₀}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{∞}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaDatatype{≡}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{y₀}\<%
\\
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{extend-preserves-basepoint}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{f}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{y₀}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{refl}\<%
\end{code}

\paragraph{Why Exactly +1?}
The universal property explains why the compactification adds \emph{exactly one} point:
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{+0 fails}: Without a basepoint, we cannot define pointed maps. The set 
$A$ itself is not pointed---there is no canonical element to serve as basepoint.
\item \textbf{+1 works}: Adding one point $\infty$ gives the \emph{minimal} pointed set 
containing $A$. This is universal: any other pointed set containing $A$ factors through $A_+$.
\item \textbf{+2 overcounts}: Adding two points would create ambiguity---which is the 
basepoint? The universal property requires a \emph{unique} basepoint.
\end{itemize}

\subsection[Why Pointedness is Forced: The D1 Theorem]{Why Pointedness is Forced: The $D_1$ Theorem}

The critic asks: why must D₀ become pointed? Why can't it remain a plain Set?

The answer: \textbf{D₁ forces pointedness}. The type-theoretic structure of our 
framework already contains a distinguished external point---the witness D₁.

Recall that D$_1$ is \emph{defined} as ``that which observes D$_0$ from outside.'' 
The record declaration \texttt{D₁ : Set} with field \texttt{from₀ : D₀} encodes 
three facts: D$_1$ exists (it is inhabited), D$_1$ references D$_0$, and D$_1$ is 
not D$_0$ (they are different types). Together, D$_1$ is a point that stands 
\emph{outside} D$_0$ while pointing \emph{to} D$_0$. This is precisely the 
pointed set structure.

The ``+1'' is not arbitrary---it is the observer. The 16 spinor states plus 
1 observer gives 17; the 4 vertices plus 1 observer gives 5; the 36 couplings 
plus 1 observer gives 37.

\paragraph{Why Exactly +1?}
All of D$_1$, D$_2$, D$_3$ can observe. Why only $+1$ and not $+3$? The answer: 
\emph{all observers collapse to a single external point}.

The ledger preserves all distinctions---D$_1$ arises from (D$_0$, D$_0$), 
D$_2$ from (D$_0$, D$_1$), and D$_3$ from (D$_0$, D$_2$). But from the perspective 
of what they observe, all observers share the same \emph{role}: they are external 
to the observed system. The one-point compactification collapses all external 
viewpoints into a single point at infinity ($\infty$).

Mathematically:
\begin{enumerate}
\item In one-point compactification, there is exactly \emph{one} point at infinity.
\item All sequences escaping to infinity converge to this single point.
\item Different observers represent different paths toward infinity.
\item But infinity itself is one point, not many.
\end{enumerate}

In conformal field theory, the point at infinity is unique even though infinitely 
many paths lead to it. The boundary of the complex plane ($\mathbb{C} \cup \{\infty\}$) 
has exactly one point, not infinitely many. The ledger tracks distinct observers, 
but the compactification has \emph{one} $\infty$.

The ledger tracks distinct observers, but the compactification has \emph{one} 
$\infty$. The path integral sums over paths, but all paths to infinity end at $\infty$.

\paragraph{The Centroid.}
When K$_4$ is embedded as a tetrahedron in $\mathbb{R}^3$, the centroid emerges as 
the 5th distinguished point, with barycentric coordinates $(1/4, 1/4, 1/4, 1/4)$.

\paragraph{The Logical Necessity.}
Why can't D₀ remain unpointed? Because \textbf{observation requires a reference frame}.
\begin{itemize}
\item To \emph{measure} a state, one needs a reference state (the vacuum, the origin, the zero).
\item To \emph{compare} states, one needs a fixed point from which to compare.
\item To \emph{observe} D₀, D₁ must exist---and D₁ IS the external point.
\end{itemize}

The "+1" is not added by us. It is \emph{already present} in the type structure: 
D₁ is the +1. We merely recognize its existence.

\paragraph{Why +1 and not +3?}
A natural question: D₁, D₂, D₃ all can observe. D₁ observes D₀; D₂ observes the 
(D₀, D₁) pair; D₃ observes the (D₀, D₂) triple. Why doesn't each observer add 
another point, giving us 16 + 3 = 19 instead of 16 + 1 = 17?

The answer: \textbf{all observers collapse to a single external point}.

The ledger preserves the genealogy---D₁, D₂, D₃ are distinct entries with distinct 
ancestry. But from the perspective of one-point compactification, they all play 
the same \emph{structural role}: they are external to the observed system. In the 
compactified space, there is exactly one point at infinity (∞), and all paths 
leading outward converge to this single point.

This is not arbitrary---it is a mathematical theorem about one-point compactification:
\begin{itemize}
\item The Alexandroff compactification of any locally compact Hausdorff space 
      adds exactly \emph{one} point.
\item In CFT, the Riemann sphere $\mathbb{C} \cup \{\infty\}$ has one point at 
      infinity, regardless of how many paths approach it.
\item The path integral sums over all paths, but the reference point for all 
      measurements is singular: one vacuum, one observer position, one ∞.
\end{itemize}

Thus: the ledger tracks all distinctions (nothing is lost), but the compactification 
collapses all external viewpoints to one (the +1). This is why 5, 17, 37---not 
7, 19, 39.

Why is this important? Consider an infinite lattice of $K_4$ cells. The 
lattice itself is unbounded, but its one-point compactification is compact. 
This compactified space has a distinguished point---the point at infinity---
where an observer can stand and "see" the entire structure at once.

This connects to several key ideas:
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{Conformal field theory}: Conformal transformations act naturally 
on compactified spaces, mapping infinity to finite points and vice versa.
\item \textbf{The witness at infinity}: The observer $D_1$ can be placed at 
the compactified point, giving a canonical "view from outside" the system.
\item \textbf{Holography}: Information about the bulk can be encoded on the 
boundary, which becomes finite after compactification.
\end{itemize}

We will return to this construction when we discuss the continuum limit and 
holographic encoding.

%% ============================================================
\chapter[The K4 Invariants]{The $K_4$ Invariants}
\label{chap:k4-invariants}
%% ============================================================

Having established that $K_4$ emerges uniquely from distinction, we now extract its 
numerical invariants. These numbers—4 vertices, 6 edges, 4 faces—are not arbitrary. 
They will determine the dimensionless constants of physics.

\paragraph{The Three Compactifications.}
The one-point compactification applies uniformly to three K$_4$ structures:

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{lccc}
\hline
\textbf{Structure} & \textbf{Base} & \textbf{Compactified} & \textbf{Physical Meaning} \\
\hline
Vertices           & $V = 4$       & $4 + 1 = 5$           & distinctions + observer \\
Spinor states      & $2^V = 16$    & $16 + 1 = 17$         & modes + vacuum \\
Edge-pair couplings& $E^2 = 36$    & $36 + 1 = 37$         & loops + tree-level \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\paragraph{What is derived, what is coincidence.}
The derivation chain is: $V=4$ (forced by endomorphism classification) $\to$ $2^V = 16$
(Clifford algebra dimension, standard construction) $\to$ $16+1 = 17$ (one-point
compactification, universal topological operation). Each step is legitimate.

What is \emph{not} derived: the number-theoretic property that $17 = 2^{2^2}+1$ is a
Fermat prime ($F_2$). There is no graph-theoretic reason why $2^V + 1$ must be prime
for $V=4$. That $17$ is prime is a fact about the natural numbers, not about $K_4$.
Similarly, $F_3 = 257 = 2^{2^3}+1 = (2^V)^2+1$ is prime, but this is a number-theoretic
coincidence. (For comparison, $F_5 = 2^{2^5}+1$ is composite.)

The physically relevant fact is: the compactified spinor count is $2^V + 1 = 17$.
Whether 17 is Fermat-prime, or merely prime, or even composite, would not change the
derivation. The Fermat label is a \emph{classification} of the derived number, not a
\emph{cause} of it.

\paragraph{Why $+1$ is Forced.}
The universal property of one-point compactification forces exactly one additional 
point. The 16 spinor states embed into the compactified space, but $\infty$ remains 
strictly outside their image.

\section{The Genesis Sequence}

The sequence $D_0 \to D_1 \to D_2 \to D_3$ is not arbitrary. Each distinction arises from the inability of previous distinctions to capture certain interactions.

\begin{figure}[h]
\centering
\begin{tikzpicture}[node distance=2cm]
  % Genesis sequence
  \node[void] (d0) {$D_0$};
  \node[unit, right=of d0] (d1) {$D_1$};
  \node[unit, right=of d1] (d2) {$D_2$};
  \node[unit, right=of d2] (d3) {$D_3$};
  
  % Labels
  \node[below=0.3cm of d0, font=\scriptsize] {break};
  \node[below=0.3cm of d1, font=\scriptsize] {polarity};
  \node[below=0.3cm of d2, font=\scriptsize] {pair};
  \node[below=0.3cm of d3, font=\scriptsize] {closure};
  
  % Arrows with reasons
  \draw[flow] (d0) -- node[above, font=\scriptsize] {self-ref} (d1);
  \draw[flow] (d1) -- node[above, font=\scriptsize] {interact} (d2);
  \draw[flow] (d2) -- node[above, font=\scriptsize] {complete} (d3);
  
  % K4 result
  \draw[->, fdAccent, thick] (d3) -- ++(1.5,0) node[right, concept] {$K_4$};
  
  % Stop marker
  \node[below=1.2cm of d1, xshift=1cm, text width=8cm, align=center, font=\small\itshape] {
    Four distinctions, no more: $K_3$ is incomplete, $K_5$ cannot embed in 3D.
  };
\end{tikzpicture}
\caption{The genesis sequence. Four distinctions arise necessarily, forming the vertices of $K_4$.}
\label{fig:genesis-sequence}
\end{figure}

$D_0$ is the first distinction---the minimal break in symmetry.
$D_1$ is the distinction of polarity---$D_0$ distinguished from itself.
$D_2$ captures the pair $(D_0, D_1)$, which was irreducible at lower levels.
$D_3$ captures the pair $(D_0, D_2)$, closing the system.

This sequence of four is forced: $K_3$ has uncaptured edges, while $K_5$ cannot embed in 3-dimensional space. Only $K_4$ is stable.
The four genesis distinctions therefore correspond to the four vertices of the complete graph $K_4$, which in turn determine the dimensionality of spacetime.

\medskip
\noindent\textit{Summary:} The genesis sequence $D_0 \to D_1 \to D_2 \to D_3$ produces exactly 
four distinctions—the four vertices of $K_4$.

%% ============================================================
\chapter{The Endomorphism Classification}
\label{chap:endomorphism-classification}
%% ============================================================

The genesis narrative of the previous chapter shows \emph{how} four vertices arise.
But a narrative is not a proof of uniqueness. Why exactly four? Why not three, or five,
or infinitely many? This chapter answers that question with a rigorous elimination
argument, adapted from a separate formal development.\footnote{The elimination core
is independently mechanically verified in a companion Agda module
(\texttt{Void/FirstDistinction.agda}).}

The strategy is to axiomatize the \emph{minimal} structure of any distinction—an
abstract type with exactly two elements—and then prove that every self-map on such
a type falls into exactly one of four cases. These four cases \emph{are} the vertices
of $K_4$. This is not a construction; it is an exhaustive classification that eliminates
all alternatives.

\section{The Abstract Distinction}

A distinction, in its most general form, is a type $S$ with exactly two elements
$\ell$ and $r$ that are provably unequal, and a coverage proof that every element
of $S$ is one of these two. This is the minimal axiomatization of ``binary choice.''

\begin{code}%
\>[0]\AgdaComment{--\ Abstract\ Distinction:\ the\ minimal\ axiom\ system\ for\ a\ 2-element\ carrier.}\<%
\\
\>[0]\AgdaComment{--\ Compare\ with\ the\ concrete\ D₂\ (which\ is\ one\ specific\ instance).}\<%
\\
\>[0]\AgdaKeyword{record}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaRecord{AbstractDistinction}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaPrimitive{Set₁}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaKeyword{where}\<%
\\
\>[0][@{}l@{\AgdaIndent{0}}]%
\>[2]\AgdaKeyword{field}\<%
\\
\>[2][@{}l@{\AgdaIndent{0}}]%
\>[4]\AgdaField{S}%
\>[10]\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaPrimitive{Set}\<%
\\
%
\>[4]\AgdaField{ad-ℓ}%
\>[10]\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{S}\<%
\\
%
\>[4]\AgdaField{ad-r}%
\>[10]\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{S}\<%
\\
%
\>[4]\AgdaField{ad-ℓ≠r}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{ad-ℓ}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaFunction{≢}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{ad-r}\<%
\\
%
\>[4]\AgdaField{ad-cover}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaBound{x}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{S}\AgdaSymbol{)}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaBound{x}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaDatatype{≡}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{ad-ℓ}\AgdaSymbol{)}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaDatatype{⊎}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaBound{x}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaDatatype{≡}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{ad-r}\AgdaSymbol{)}\<%
\\
%
\\[\AgdaEmptyExtraSkip]%
\>[0]\AgdaKeyword{open}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaModule{AbstractDistinction}\<%
\end{code}

The coverage axiom is the key: it says there is \emph{nothing else} in $S$ besides
$\ell$ and $r$. From this single axiom, all subsequent structure is forced.

\paragraph{Bool is an AbstractDistinction.}
Our existing type \texttt{Bool} with constructors \texttt{true} and \texttt{false}
is a canonical instance. This bridges the abstract theory to the concrete types
already established.

\begin{code}%
\>[0]\AgdaComment{--\ Coverage\ for\ Bool:\ each\ value\ is\ true\ or\ false.}\<%
\\

\AgdaOperator{\AgdaDatatype{≡}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{true}\AgdaSymbol{)}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaDatatype{⊎}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaBound{b}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaDatatype{≡}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{false}\AgdaSymbol{)}\<%
\\
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{Bool-cover}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{true}%
\>[17]\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{inj₁}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{refl}\<%
\\

\AgdaInductiveConstructor{false}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{inj₂}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{refl}\<%
\\
%
\\[\AgdaEmptyExtraSkip]%

\\
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{Bool-distinction}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaRecord{AbstractDistinction}\<%
\\
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{Bool-distinction}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaKeyword{record}\<%
\\
\>[0][@{}l@{\AgdaIndent{0}}]%
\>[2]\AgdaSymbol{\{}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{S}%
\>[11]\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaDatatype{Bool}\<%
\\
%
\>[2]\AgdaSymbol{;}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{ad-ℓ}%
\>[11]\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%

%

%

\AgdaField{ad-ℓ≠r}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{true≢false}\<%
\\
%
\>[2]\AgdaSymbol{;}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{ad-cover}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{Bool-cover}\<%
\\
%
\>[2]\AgdaSymbol{\}}\<%
\end{code}

\section{The Elimination Lemma}

From coverage, we derive the fundamental elimination principle: any property that
holds at $\ell$ and at $r$ holds everywhere on $S$.

\begin{code}%
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{AD-elim}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}%
\>[17678I]\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaBound{d}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaRecord{AbstractDistinction}\AgdaSymbol{)}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\<%
\\
\>[.][@{}l@{}]\<[17678I]%
\>[10]\AgdaSymbol{\{}\AgdaBound{P}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{S}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{d}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaPrimitive{Set}\AgdaSymbol{\}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\<%
\\
%
\>[10]\AgdaBound{P}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaField{ad-ℓ}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{d}\AgdaSymbol{)}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{P}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaField{ad-r}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{d}\AgdaSymbol{)}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\<%
\\
%
\>[10]\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaBound{x}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{S}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{d}\AgdaSymbol{)}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{P}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{x}\<%
\\
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{AD-elim}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{d}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{pℓ}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{pr}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{x}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaKeyword{with}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{ad-cover}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{d}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{x}\<%
\\
\>[0]\AgdaSymbol{...}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{|}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{inj₁}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{x≡ℓ}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{subst}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{\AgdaUnderscore{}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaFunction{sym}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{x≡ℓ}\AgdaSymbol{)}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{pℓ}\<%
\\
\>[0]\AgdaSymbol{...}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{|}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{inj₂}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{x≡r}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{subst}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{\AgdaUnderscore{}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaFunction{sym}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{x≡r}\AgdaSymbol{)}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{pr}\<%
\end{code}

This is not a definition—it is a \emph{forced consequence} of coverage. If every
element is $\ell$ or $r$, then every predicate is determined by its values at those
two points. No alternative is possible.

\paragraph{Boundary determination for functions.}
Any function out of $S$ is completely determined by its values at $\ell$ and $r$.

\begin{code}%
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{AD-determined}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}%
\>[17728I]\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaBound{d}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaRecord{AbstractDistinction}\AgdaSymbol{)}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\<%
\\
\>[.][@{}l@{}]\<[17728I]%
\>[16]\AgdaSymbol{\{}\AgdaBound{B}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaPrimitive{Set}\AgdaSymbol{\}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\<%
\\
%
\>[16]\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaBound{f}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{g}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{S}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{d}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{B}\AgdaSymbol{)}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\<%
\\
%
\>[16]\AgdaBound{f}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaField{ad-ℓ}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{d}\AgdaSymbol{)}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaDatatype{≡}}\AgdaSpace{}%

\AgdaField{S}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{d}\AgdaSymbol{)}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{f}\AgdaSpace{}%

\AgdaOperator{\AgdaDatatype{≡}}\AgdaSpace{}%

\\

\AgdaBound{d}\AgdaSpace{}%

\AgdaBound{d}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{eqℓ}\AgdaSpace{}%

\end{code}

\paragraph{Duality.}
Every abstract distinction admits a canonical involution: swap $\ell$ and $r$.

\section{The Four Endomorphism Cases}
\label{sec:endocase}

We now prove the central classification theorem. Every endomorphism
$f : S \to S$ on an abstract distinction falls into exactly one of four cases,
determined by the pair $(f(\ell), f(r))$.

\begin{code}%
\>[0]\AgdaComment{--\ The\ four\ endomorphism\ types.\ These\ ARE\ the\ K₄\ vertices.}\<%
\\
\>[0]\AgdaKeyword{data}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaDatatype{EndoCase}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaPrimitive{Set}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaKeyword{where}\<%
\\
\>[0][@{}l@{\AgdaIndent{0}}]%
\>[2]\AgdaInductiveConstructor{case-constL}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaDatatype{EndoCase}%
\>[27]\AgdaComment{--\ f\ sends\ everything\ to\ ℓ}\<%
\\
%
\>[2]\AgdaInductiveConstructor{case-constR}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaDatatype{EndoCase}%
\>[27]\AgdaComment{--\ f\ sends\ everything\ to\ r}\<%
\\
%
\>[2]\AgdaInductiveConstructor{case-id}%
\>[14]\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaDatatype{EndoCase}%
\>[27]\AgdaComment{--\ f\ preserves\ sides\ (identity-like)}\<%
\\
%
\>[2]\AgdaInductiveConstructor{case-dual}%
\>[14]\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaDatatype{EndoCase}%
\>[27]\AgdaComment{--\ f\ swaps\ sides\ (duality)}\<%
\end{code}

\paragraph{Why exactly four?}
A function $f : S \to S$ is determined by two values: $f(\ell)$ and $f(r)$.
Each value must be either $\ell$ or $r$ (by coverage). This gives $2 \times 2 = 4$
possibilities, each realized by one constructor:
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{lccl}
\toprule
\textbf{Case} & $f(\ell)$ & $f(r)$ & \textbf{Meaning} \\
\midrule
\texttt{case-constL} & $\ell$ & $\ell$ & constant left \\
\texttt{case-constR} & $r$ & $r$ & constant right \\
\texttt{case-id}     & $\ell$ & $r$ & identity \\
\texttt{case-dual}   & $r$ & $\ell$ & duality \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

This is exhaustive. There is no fifth option because $f(\ell) \in \{\ell, r\}$
and $f(r) \in \{\ell, r\}$, and $2 \times 2 = 4$.

\subsection{The Endomorphism Module}

Fix an arbitrary abstract distinction. All endomorphism classification takes place
within this fixed context---the classification depends on the distinction, not on
which particular one.

\begin{code}%
\>[0]\AgdaKeyword{module}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaModule{EndoModule}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaBound{d}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaRecord{AbstractDistinction}\AgdaSymbol{)}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaKeyword{where}\<%
\\
%
\\[\AgdaEmptyExtraSkip]%
\>[0][@{}l@{\AgdaIndent{0}}]%
\>[2]\AgdaFunction{interpret}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaDatatype{EndoCase}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaField{S}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{d}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{S}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{d}\AgdaSymbol{)}\<%
\\
%
\>[2]\AgdaFunction{interpret}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{case-constL}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{\AgdaUnderscore{}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{ad-ℓ}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{d}\<%
\\
%
\>[2]\AgdaFunction{interpret}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{case-constR}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{\AgdaUnderscore{}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{ad-r}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{d}\<%
\\
%
\>[2]\AgdaFunction{interpret}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{case-id}%
\>[24]\AgdaBound{x}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{x}\<%
\\
%
\>[2]\AgdaFunction{interpret}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{case-dual}%
\>[26]\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{AD-dual}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{d}\<%
\end{code}

\subsection{Classification: From Function to Case}

Given any endomorphism, we classify it by inspecting its boundary values:

\begin{code}%
%
\>[2]\AgdaFunction{classify}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaField{S}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{d}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{S}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{d}\AgdaSymbol{)}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaDatatype{EndoCase}\<%
\\
%
\>[2]\AgdaFunction{classify}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{f}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaKeyword{with}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{ad-cover}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{d}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaBound{f}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaField{ad-ℓ}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{d}\AgdaSymbol{))}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{|}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{ad-cover}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{d}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaBound{f}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaField{ad-r}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{d}\AgdaSymbol{))}\<%
\\
%
\>[2]\AgdaSymbol{...}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{|}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{inj₁}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{\AgdaUnderscore{}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{|}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{inj₁}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{\AgdaUnderscore{}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{case-constL}\<%
\\
%
\>[2]\AgdaSymbol{...}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{|}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{inj₂}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{\AgdaUnderscore{}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{|}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{inj₂}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{\AgdaUnderscore{}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{case-constR}\<%
\\
%
\>[2]\AgdaSymbol{...}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{|}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{inj₁}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{\AgdaUnderscore{}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{|}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{inj₂}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{\AgdaUnderscore{}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{case-id}\<%
\\
%
\>[2]\AgdaSymbol{...}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{|}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{inj₂}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{\AgdaUnderscore{}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{|}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{inj₁}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{\AgdaUnderscore{}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{case-dual}\<%
\end{code}

\subsection{Soundness: Classification Recovers the Function}

The composition $\texttt{interpret} \circ \texttt{classify}$ is pointwise
equal to the original function. This is the \emph{soundness} of the classification.

\begin{code}%
%
\>[2]\AgdaFunction{sound-at-ℓ}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}%
\>[17957I]\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaBound{f}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{S}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{d}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{S}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{d}\AgdaSymbol{)}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\<%
\\
\>[.][@{}l@{}]\<[17957I]%
\>[15]\AgdaFunction{interpret}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaFunction{classify}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{f}\AgdaSymbol{)}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaField{ad-ℓ}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{d}\AgdaSymbol{)}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaDatatype{≡}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{f}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaField{ad-ℓ}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{d}\AgdaSymbol{)}\<%
\\
%

\AgdaBound{d}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaBound{f}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaField{ad-r}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{d}\AgdaSymbol{))}\<%
\\
%
\>[2]\AgdaSymbol{...}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{|}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{inj₁}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{fl≡ℓ}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{|}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{inj₁}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{\AgdaUnderscore{}}%

\>[2]\AgdaSymbol{...}\AgdaSpace{}%

\\
%
\\[\AgdaEmptyExtraSkip]%
%
\>[2]\AgdaFunction{sound-at-r}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}%
\>[18022I]\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaBound{f}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%

\>[.][@{}l@{}]\<[18022I]%
\>[15]\AgdaFunction{interpret}\AgdaSpace{}%

\subsection{Injectivity: Distinct Cases Give Distinct Functions}

If two cases produce pointwise-equal functions, they must be the same case.
This eliminates representational ambiguity.

\begin{code}%
%

\subsection{The Classification Theorem}

We can now state the full result: EndoCase is a \emph{complete, sound, and unique}
classifier for endomorphisms on any abstract distinction. Every function $S \to S$
has a canonical representative, and that representative is unique.

\begin{code}%
%
\>[2]\AgdaComment{--\ Every\ endomorphism\ has\ a\ witness\ in\ EndoCase\ (existence\ +\ soundness)}\<%
\\
%
\>[2]\AgdaFunction{endo-witness}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}%
\>[18275I]\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaBound{f}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{S}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{d}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{S}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{d}\AgdaSymbol{)}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\<%
\\

\>[17]\AgdaRecord{Σ}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaDatatype{EndoCase}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(λ}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{c}\AgdaSpace{}%

\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%

\medskip
\noindent\textbf{This is the core result.} The four EndoCase constructors are not
postulated. They are \emph{forced}: any abstract distinction with two elements has
exactly four self-maps, classified exhaustively and uniquely. No fifth case can exist
(there is no fifth boundary-value pair). No case can be removed (each is realized by
a concrete function). The cardinality 4 is not assumed—it is derived from the
elimination principle.

\section[EndoCase Is K4]{EndoCase Is $K_4$}
\label{sec:endocase-is-k4}

The four endomorphism cases are the four vertices of $K_4$. An edge between two
vertices means the corresponding endomorphisms are \emph{distinct}---they differ on
at least one input. Since all four cases produce distinct functions (by injectivity),
every pair of distinct cases forms an edge. This is the complete graph $K_4$.

\begin{code}%

\paragraph{Edge = Inequality.}
Two EndoCase values that are \emph{not equal} constitute an edge. This is the
canonical graph on EndoCase—and it is $K_4$, because every pair of distinct
cases is connected.

\begin{code}%
\>[0]\AgdaComment{--\ K₄\ edge:\ any\ two\ distinct\ endomorphism\ cases}\<%
\\
\>[0]\AgdaKeyword{record}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaRecord{EndoEdge}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaPrimitive{Set}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaKeyword{where}\<%
\\
\>[0][@{}l@{\AgdaIndent{0}}]%
\>[2]\AgdaKeyword{constructor}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{mk-endo-edge}\<%
\\
%
\>[2]\AgdaKeyword{field}\<%
\\
\>[2][@{}l@{\AgdaIndent{0}}]%
\>[4]\AgdaField{endo-src}%
\>[14]\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaDatatype{EndoCase}\<%
\\
%
\>[4]\AgdaField{endo-tgt}%
\>[14]\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaDatatype{EndoCase}\<%
\\
%
\>[4]\AgdaField{endo-neq}%
\>[14]\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{endo-src}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaFunction{≢}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{endo-tgt}\<%
\\
%
\\[\AgdaEmptyExtraSkip]%
\>[0]\AgdaComment{--\ All\ six\ edges,\ by\ exhaustion}\<%
\\
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{endo-edge-CL-CR}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%

\section{Simplex Invariants from Structure}
\label{sec:simplex-invariants-derived}

The simplex invariants—$V = 4$, $d = 3$, $E = 6$, $\chi = 2$—are now
\emph{derived} from the endomorphism classification, not postulated.

\begin{code}%

\section{Bridge to Genesis: EndoCase ≅ GenesisID}
\label{sec:endo-genesis-bridge}

The abstract endomorphism cases and the concrete genesis IDs are isomorphic.
This connects the elimination argument (why exactly four) with the genesis
narrative (how the four arise). Both arrive at the same structure—by
different but convergent paths.

\section{Presentation Uniqueness}
\label{sec:presentation-unique}

The endomorphism classification does not depend on the choice of abstract distinction.
If \emph{any} type with two elements is used, the same four cases arise, and any
alternative presentation (with its own classify/interpret pair) is isomorphic to
EndoCase. This eliminates representational freedom.

\section{Maps Between Distinctions}
\label{sec:maps-between}

The classification extends from endomorphisms ($S \to S$) to \emph{heteromorphisms}
($S_1 \to S_2$) between any two abstract distinctions. Every such map is also determined
by its boundary values and falls into the same four cases.

\medskip
\noindent\textbf{What this chapter proves:}
\begin{enumerate}
\item The abstract structure of distinction (a type with two elements) \emph{forces}
exactly four endomorphisms.
\item These four cases are the vertices of $K_4$.
\item The edge relation (inequality) makes the graph complete.
\item The classification is presentation-independent: any equivalent axiomatization
yields the same four cases, up to isomorphism.
\item The simplex invariants ($V=4$, $d=3$, $E=6$, $\chi=2$) are derived, not assumed.
\item The genesis IDs of the previous chapter are isomorphic to EndoCase, confirming
that the narrative and the elimination argument converge.
\end{enumerate}

This eliminates the gap identified in the genesis narrative: the four vertices are not
merely counted—they are \emph{classified exhaustively} from the minimal structure of
distinction itself.

%% ============================================================
\chapter[Why K4 is Complete]{Why $K_4$ is Complete}
\label{chap:why-complete}
%% ============================================================

We have shown that $K_4$ has four vertices. But why is the graph \emph{complete}—why 
does every pair of vertices share an edge? This chapter proves that completeness 
is forced by the witnessing requirement.

\section{Why Binary Relations Are Forced}
\label{sec:binary-forced}

Before counting pairs, we must address a prior question: why are the relations 
between distinctions \emph{binary}? Why use a graph (2-relations) rather than a 
hypergraph (3-relations, 4-relations, \ldots)?

The answer lies in the nature of distinction itself. \textbf{To distinguish is to 
separate \emph{this} from \emph{that}.} The act of distinction is irreducibly binary: 
it cleaves one thing from another. There is no ternary distinction---no simultaneous, 
atomic separation of three things that is not reducible to sequential binary ones.

\paragraph{The Temporal Reduction Theorem.}
Consider a putative ternary relation $R(a, b, c)$---a simultaneous interaction of 
three entities. For this relation to \emph{exist} in our framework (i.e., to be 
witnessed), an observer must distinguish all three participants. But observation is 
sequential: the witness must first distinguish $a$ from $b$, then distinguish the 
composite $\{a, b\}$ from $c$. The ternary relation decomposes over time:
\[
R(a, b, c) \;\longrightarrow\; R_1(a, b) \;\text{then}\; R_2(\{a,b\}, c)
\]
This is not a limitation of our formalism---it is a consequence of the structure 
of distinction. The $D_0 \to D_1$ step (Chapter 2) already encoded this: the witness 
observes \emph{one} thing at a time. The cut $D_2$ (Chapter 3) introduced \emph{two} 
sides---not three, not four. Duality is the arity of distinction.

\paragraph{Why This Cannot Be Otherwise.}
\begin{itemize}
\item A \textbf{unary} relation (self-relation) is not a distinction at all---it is 
identity. Self-reference ($D_0$ observing $D_0$) produces $D_1$, but $D_1$ is 
already a \emph{pair} (observer, observed).
\item A \textbf{ternary} relation $R(a,b,c)$ presupposes that the witness can hold 
three things in view simultaneously. But the witness is $D_1$---a single point with a 
single field (\texttt{from₀ : D₀}). It sees one thing. To see three, it must 
distinguish sequentially, which reduces to binary steps.
\item More generally, any \textbf{$n$-ary} relation for $n \geq 3$ factors into 
$n - 1$ binary relations over time. This is the content of the statement ``distinction 
is binary.''
\end{itemize}

Therefore the interaction structure is a \emph{graph} (binary edges), not a hypergraph. 
The triangular number $T_n$ correctly counts the possible interactions.

\begin{code}%
\>[0]\AgdaComment{--\ Ternary\ relations\ reduce\ to\ sequential\ binary\ distinctions.}\<%
\\
\>[0]\AgdaComment{--\ A\ ternary\ distinction\ R(a,b,c)\ decomposes\ into:}\<%
\\
\>[0]\AgdaComment{--\ \ \ step\ 1:\ distinguish\ a\ from\ b\ \ (binary)}\<%
\\
\>[0]\AgdaComment{--\ \ \ step\ 2:\ distinguish\ \{a,b\}\ from\ c\ \ (binary)}\<%
\\
\>[0]\AgdaComment{--\ This\ is\ forced\ by\ the\ structure\ of\ D₁,\ which\ has\ a\ single\ field.}\<%
\\
%

\section{Triangular Numbers and Combinatorics}

The triangular number $T_n = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} k = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ counts the number of distinct pairs in a set of $n$ elements—the fundamental combinatorics of interaction.

\section{The Completeness Argument}

\textbf{Why must the graph be complete?} This is not a choice. The argument is:
\begin{enumerate}
\item Any two distinctions $D_i, D_j$ \emph{can} interact (there is no ``forbidden'' combination)
\item An interaction between $D_i$ and $D_j$ must be \emph{witnessed} (recorded in the structure)
\item A witness is a third element that ``sees'' the pair---this creates an edge $(D_i, D_j)$
\item Therefore: every possible pair has an edge
\item A graph where every pair has an edge is, by definition, \emph{complete}
\end{enumerate}

The completeness is not imposed from outside. It follows from the requirement that 
\textbf{all interactions are witnessed}. A non-complete graph would have ``unwitnessed pairs''---relations 
that exist but are not recorded. This violates the closure condition.

In a system with $n$ distinguishable entities, there are $T_n$ possible binary interactions (edges in the graph).
For $K_4$, we have $T_4 = 6$ edges, which matches the observed structure.

We call this function \texttt{memory} because each interaction leaves a trace, a record of the relation between two distinctions.
The saturation condition---when all pairs are witnessed---determines the closure of the ontological structure.

\AgdaInductiveConstructor{refl}\<%
\end{code}

\noindent Note: This is deliberately just an arithmetic identity ($5 = 4 + 1$).
The \emph{substantive} argument for why $K_5$ cannot arise is not dimensional
(which would be circular) but algebraic: a 2-element type has exactly 4
endomorphisms (Chapter~\ref{chap:endomorphism-classification}), so there is no
fifth vertex to add.  The genesis closure argument (all 6 edges are
witnessed $\Rightarrow$ no forcing for $D_4$) provides the dynamical reason;
the endomorphism classification provides the structural one.  Together they
make the termination at $K_4$ non-circular.

\subsection{Levels of Emergence}

Distinctions do not all occupy the same ontological level. They emerge in layers:
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{Foundation} ($D_0$): The first distinction, the ground.
\item \textbf{Polarity} ($D_1$): The distinction between $D_0$ and its negation.
\item \textbf{Closure} ($D_2$): The distinction that captures $(D_0, D_1)$.
\item \textbf{Meta-level} ($D_3$): The distinction that witnesses irreducible pairs from lower levels.
\end{itemize}

This hierarchy is not imposed from outside---it arises from the internal logic of the structure.
Each level is forced by the incompleteness of the previous level.

\begin{code}%
\>[0]\AgdaKeyword{data}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaDatatype{EmergenceLevel}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaPrimitive{Set}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaKeyword{where}\<%
\\

\AgdaDatatype{EmergenceLevel}\<%
\\

\\
%

\subsection{The Capture Relation}

The \emph{capture} relation formalizes when a distinction $d$ "contains" or "witnesses" a pair $(a, b)$.

Formally, $d$ captures $(a, b)$ if:
\begin{itemize}
\item $(a, b)$ is reflexive (both equal to $d$), or
\item $(a, b)$ is the defining pair for $d$ (e.g., $(D_0, D_1)$ defines $D_2$), or
\item $(a, b)$ involves $d$ directly (e.g., $(D_3, x)$ for any $x$).
\end{itemize}

This relation is computable (we provide a Boolean function \texttt{captures?}) and exhaustive.
Every pair is either captured by some existing distinction, or forces the creation of a new one.

\AgdaSymbol{→}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaDatatype{Bool}\<%
\\
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{captures?}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{is-computed-witness}\<%

\section{Irreducible Pairs and Forcing}

An irreducible pair is a relation between two distinctions that cannot be expressed in terms of existing distinctions.
The pair $(D_0, D_2)$ is irreducible: it cannot be captured by $D_0$, $D_1$, or $D_2$ alone.

The existence of an irreducible pair \emph{forces} the emergence of a new distinction.
This is the logical analogue of forcing in set theory: the consistency of the existing structure demands an extension.

Without $D_3$ to witness $(D_0, D_2)$, the ontology would be incomplete.
The graph would have an "open edge,'' a relation without a container.
The forcing mechanism ensures closure: every pair is eventually witnessed, and the structure stabilizes at $K_4$.

With $K_4$ complete, there is no forcing for a fifth distinction $D_4$.

The argument has two independent legs, and neither is circular:

\begin{enumerate}
\item \textbf{Algebraic (from EndoModule):} Any abstract distinction has exactly four
      endomorphisms (Chapter~\ref{chap:endomorphism-classification}). This is a
      theorem about 2-element types, independent of dimension. The number 4 arises
      because $f(\ell) \in \{\ell, r\}$ and $f(r) \in \{\ell, r\}$, giving
      $2 \times 2 = 4$. A fifth endomorphism would require a fifth boundary-value
      pair, which does not exist.

\item \textbf{Witness-Closure (from Genesis):} The genesis sequence
      $D_0 \to D_1 \to D_2 \to D_3$ terminates because all six edges of $K_4$
      are witnessed. A new distinction $D_4$ would be forced only if an
      unwitnessed pair existed. Since every pair among $\{D_0, D_1, D_2, D_3\}$
      is already witnessed, no forcing remains.
\end{enumerate}

Crucially, the dimension $d = 3$ is a \emph{consequence} of $|K_4| = 4$ (via the
Laplacian eigenspace multiplicity), not a premise. The logical order is:
\[
  \text{2-element type} \;\Rightarrow\; 4\text{ endomorphisms} \;\Rightarrow\;
  K_4 \;\Rightarrow\; \lambda_1 = 4\text{ with multiplicity }3 \;\Rightarrow\; d = 3.
\]
The dimension is derived, not assumed.

This proves the uniqueness of $K_4$ as the foundational structure. The proof
has three independent components: $K_3$ is \emph{too small} (unwitnessed edges
remain), $K_4$ is \emph{just right} (all edges witnessed), and $K_5$ is
\emph{algebraically impossible} (there is no fifth endomorphism of a 2-element type).

\medskip
\noindent\textit{Summary:} $K_4$ is forced: $K_3$ is too small (incomplete witnessing), 
$K_5$ is unreachable (no forcing for $D_4$), and $K_4$ exactly satisfies all constraints.

%% ============================================================
\chapter[Eigenvalues of K4]{Eigenvalues of $K_4$}
\label{chap:spectral-theory}
%% ============================================================

Having established that $K_4$ is unique, we now turn to its \emph{spectral structure}. 
The eigenvalues of the Laplacian matrix encode the geometry of the graph—and, as we shall 
see, the geometry of physical space. This chapter derives the spectrum and shows how 
spatial dimension emerges.

\section{Global Classification of Complete Graphs}
\label{sec:global-classification}

Having established the structural properties of $K_4$, we now prove the \textbf{global uniqueness theorem}: for \textbf{all} complete graphs $K_n$, the value $n = 4$ is the unique solution to the witness-closure and dimensional constraints.

This is the foundational theorem upon which all subsequent physics depends. The argument has three parts:
\begin{enumerate}
\item \textbf{Too small} ($n < 4$): Insufficient vertices to close all witness relations
\item \textbf{Exactly right} ($n = 4$): All pairs witnessed, no forcing for additional vertices
\item \textbf{Unreachable} ($n > 4$): No logical mechanism forces a fifth distinction
\end{enumerate}

The impossibility proofs follow a uniform pattern: for each $n \neq 4$, we exhibit a constraint violation. 
For $n < 4$, there are too few edges to close all witness relations.
For $n > 4$, no forcing mechanism exists---the structure is already complete at $n = 4$.

$K_3$ (the triangle) has only 3 edges and embeds in 2 dimensions. It cannot satisfy the witness-closure constraint, which requires 6 edges. The graph is \emph{too flat}.

For $n \geq 5$, the situation is different: the constraint is not violated, but there is no \emph{forcing mechanism}. Once $K_4$ is complete, all pairs are witnessed---all six edges are captured. There is no ``uncaptured pair'' that would force a fifth distinction into existence.

We now state the \textbf{Global Classification Theorem}: $K_4$ is the unique complete graph satisfying the witness-closure constraint.

\textbf{The Master Uniqueness Theorem.} The theorem \texttt{theorem-4-unique-fixpoint} is the single, global $\forall$-statement that carries the uniqueness claim:

\begin{quote}
\emph{For all $n \in \mathbb{N}$: if $K_n$ has exactly 6 edges and degree 3, then $n = 4$.}
\end{quote}

This is a genuine universal quantification over all natural numbers, verified by Agda's coverage checker. \textbf{All subsequent physics---the fine-structure constant, particle masses, cosmological parameters---flows from this single mathematical fact.}

We enumerate the genesis IDs to prove their cardinality.

The genesis sequence forces the emergence of the $K_4$ graph.

\begin{code}%
\>[0]\AgdaKeyword{record}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaRecord{GenesisForcessK4}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaPrimitive{Set}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaKeyword{where}\<%
\\

\\

\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaRecord{GenesisBijection}\<%
\\
%

\AgdaOperator{\AgdaDatatype{≡}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaNumber{4}\<%
\\
%

%

\\
\>[0]\<%
\end{code}

The proof is completed by instantiating the record with our established theorems.

\begin{code}%
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{theorem-D0-forces-K4}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaRecord{GenesisForcessK4}\<%
\\
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{theorem-D0-forces-K4}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaKeyword{record}\<%
\\
\>[0][@{}l@{\AgdaIndent{0}}]%
\>[2]\AgdaSymbol{\{}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{genesis-count-4}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{theorem-genesis-has-exactly-4}\<%
\\
%
\>[2]\AgdaSymbol{;}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{K4-vertex-count-4}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{refl}\<%
\\
%
\>[2]\AgdaSymbol{;}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{vertex-is-genesis}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{theorem-vertices-are-genesis}\<%
\\
%
\>[2]\AgdaSymbol{;}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{edge-is-pair}\AgdaSpace{}%

\\
%
\>[2]\AgdaSymbol{;}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{K4-forced}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{theorem-K4-uniqueness-complete}\<%
\\
%
\>[2]\AgdaSymbol{\}}\<%
\end{code}

\medskip
\noindent\textit{Summary:} The chain is complete: $D_0 \to$ genesis sequence $\to$ 4 vertices 
$\to$ witness closure $\to$ 6 edges $\to K_4$. The graph is forced.

%% ============================================================
\chapter[Spectral Theory of K4]{Spectral Theory of $K_4$}
\label{chap:spectral-k4}
%% ============================================================

With the graph $K_4$ established, we now enter spectral analysis. The eigenvalues of the 
Laplacian matrix are not just abstract numbers—they determine the embedding dimension 
of physical space and the structure of quantum states.

\section{The Texture of Connection}

Not all edges in the graph are born equal; some represent established relationships, while others represent the breaking of new ground---irreducible distinctions.

\begin{code}%
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{genesis-pair-status}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaDatatype{GenesisID}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaDatatype{GenesisID}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaDatatype{PairStatus}\<%
\\
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{genesis-pair-status}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{classify-pair}\<%
\\
\>[0]\<%
\end{code}

The total number of distinct pairs in a 4-element set is $\binom{4}{2} = 6$.

\begin{code}%
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{count-distinct-pairs}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaDatatype{ℕ}\<%
\\
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{count-distinct-pairs}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{suc}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaInductiveConstructor{suc}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaInductiveConstructor{suc}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaInductiveConstructor{suc}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaInductiveConstructor{suc}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaInductiveConstructor{suc}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{zero}\AgdaSymbol{)))))}\<%
\\
\>[0]\<%
\end{code}

This matches the edge count of $K_4$.

\begin{code}%
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{theorem-edges-from-genesis-pairs}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{k4-edge-count}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaDatatype{≡}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{count-distinct-pairs}\<%
\\
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{theorem-edges-from-genesis-pairs}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{refl}\<%
\\
\>[0]\<%
\end{code}

We can inspect the status of each specific pair of distinctions. This classification reveals the internal logic of the genesis sequence.

\begin{code}%
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{theorem-edge-01-classified}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{classify-pair}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{D₀-id}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{D₁-id}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaDatatype{≡}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{already-exists}\<%
\\
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{theorem-edge-01-classified}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{refl}\<%
\\
%
\\[\AgdaEmptyExtraSkip]%

\section{Spectral Geometry of the Void}

To do physics, we need a metric. In graph theory, the metric structure is encoded in the Laplacian matrix. We begin by defining equality and adjacency on the vertices.

\begin{figure}[h]
\centering
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.9]
  % Laplacian eigenvalue spectrum
  \draw[->, fdGray, thick] (0,0) -- (6,0) node[right] {$\lambda$};
  \draw[->, fdGray, thick] (0,0) -- (0,3) node[above] {mult};
  
  % Eigenvalues
  \draw[fdBlue, very thick] (0.5,0) -- (0.5,2);
  \node[below] at (0.5,0) {$0$};
  \node[right, font=\scriptsize] at (0.5,1.8) {$\times 1$};
  
  \draw[fdAccent, very thick] (4,0) -- (4,2.5);
  \node[below] at (4,0) {$4$};
  \node[right, font=\scriptsize] at (4,2.3) {$\times 3$};
  
  % Interpretation
  \node[below=1.5cm, text width=6cm, align=center] at (3,0) {
    $K_4$ Laplacian: $\lambda_0 = 0$ (connectedness),\\
    $\lambda_{1,2,3} = 4$ (curvature $= 12$)
  };
  
  % Arrow to dimension
  \draw[->, fdBlue, thick] (5.5,2) -- (7,2);
  \node[right] at (7,2) {$d = 3$};
\end{tikzpicture}
\caption{Spectral geometry of $K_4$. The eigenvalue spectrum determines both curvature and dimension.}
\label{fig:spectral-geometry}
\end{figure}

The degree of a vertex is the number of edges connected to it. In $K_4$, every vertex is connected to every other vertex, so the degree is always 3.

\AgdaBound{i}\<%

The Laplacian matrix $L$ is defined as $D - A$, where $D$ is the degree matrix and $A$ is the adjacency matrix. This operator describes how a quantity diffuses across the graph.

\begin{code}%
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{Laplacian}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaDatatype{K4Vertex}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaDatatype{K4Vertex}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaRecord{ℤ}\<%
\\
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{Laplacian}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{i}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{j}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{natToℤ}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaFunction{DegreeMatrix}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{i}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{j}\AgdaSymbol{)}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaFunction{+ℤ}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{negℤ}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaFunction{natToℤ}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaFunction{Adjacency}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{i}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{j}\AgdaSymbol{))}\<%
\end{code}

\section{The Eigenvalue Problem}

The spectrum of the Laplacian reveals the fundamental frequencies of the graph. We define an eigenvector as a function from vertices to integers (since we are working in constructive integer arithmetic).

\begin{code}%
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{Eigenvector}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaPrimitive{Set}\<%
\\
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{Eigenvector}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaDatatype{K4Vertex}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaRecord{ℤ}\<%
\\
%
\\[\AgdaEmptyExtraSkip]%
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{applyLaplacian}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{Eigenvector}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{Eigenvector}\<%
\\
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{applyLaplacian}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{ev}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{λ}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{v}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\<%
\\
\>[0][@{}l@{\AgdaIndent{0}}]%
\>[2]\AgdaSymbol{((}\AgdaFunction{Laplacian}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{v}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{v₀}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaFunction{*ℤ}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{ev}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{v₀}\AgdaSymbol{)}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaFunction{+ℤ}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaFunction{Laplacian}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{v}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{v₁}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaFunction{*ℤ}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{ev}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{v₁}\AgdaSymbol{))}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaFunction{+ℤ}}\<%
\\
%
\>[2]\AgdaSymbol{((}\AgdaFunction{Laplacian}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{v}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{v₂}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaFunction{*ℤ}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{ev}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{v₂}\AgdaSymbol{)}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaFunction{+ℤ}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaFunction{Laplacian}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{v}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{v₃}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaFunction{*ℤ}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{ev}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{v₃}\AgdaSymbol{))}\<%
\\
%
\\[\AgdaEmptyExtraSkip]%
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{scaleEigenvector}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaRecord{ℤ}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{Eigenvector}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{Eigenvector}\<%
\\
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{scaleEigenvector}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{scalar}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{ev}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{λ}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{v}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{→}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{scalar}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaFunction{*ℤ}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{ev}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaBound{v}\<%
\end{code}

For the complete graph $K_4$, the Laplacian has a degenerate eigenvalue $\lambda = 4$ with multiplicity 3. This number 4 is not arbitrary; it is the number of vertices.

\begin{code}%
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{λ₄}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaRecord{ℤ}\<%
\\
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{λ₄}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{mkℤ}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaInductiveConstructor{suc}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaInductiveConstructor{suc}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaInductiveConstructor{suc}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaInductiveConstructor{suc}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{zero}\AgdaSymbol{))))}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{zero}\<%
\\
\>[0]\<%
\end{code}

We can explicitly construct three linearly independent eigenvectors corresponding to this eigenvalue. These vectors span the "space" of the graph.

\begin{code}%
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{eigenvector-1}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{Eigenvector}\<%
\\

\AgdaFunction{1ℤ}\<%
\\

\AgdaInductiveConstructor{v₁}\AgdaSpace{}%

\AgdaFunction{-1ℤ}\<%
\\

\AgdaInductiveConstructor{v₂}\AgdaSpace{}%

\AgdaFunction{0ℤ}\<%
\\

\AgdaInductiveConstructor{v₃}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{0ℤ}\<%
\\
%

\AgdaFunction{Eigenvector}\<%

\\[\AgdaEmptyExtraSkip]%

Each eigenvector encodes a ``direction'' in spectral space. The fact that all three satisfy the eigenvector equation for $\lambda = 4$ is not assumed---it is computed. Agda verifies each case by definitional equality.

We collect these results into a consistency record.

\begin{code}%
\>[0]\AgdaKeyword{record}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaRecord{EigenspaceConsistency}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaPrimitive{Set}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaKeyword{where}\<%
\\
\>[0][@{}l@{\AgdaIndent{0}}]%
\>[2]\AgdaKeyword{field}\<%
\\
\>[2][@{}l@{\AgdaIndent{0}}]%
\>[4]\AgdaField{ev1-satisfies}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{IsEigenvector}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{eigenvector-1}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{λ₄}\<%
\\
%
\>[4]\AgdaField{ev2-satisfies}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{IsEigenvector}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{eigenvector-2}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{λ₄}\<%
\\
%
\>[4]\AgdaField{ev3-satisfies}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{IsEigenvector}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{eigenvector-3}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{λ₄}\<%
\\
%
\\[\AgdaEmptyExtraSkip]%
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{theorem-eigenspace-consistent}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaRecord{EigenspaceConsistency}\<%
\\
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{theorem-eigenspace-consistent}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaKeyword{record}\<%
\\
\>[0][@{}l@{\AgdaIndent{0}}]%
\>[2]\AgdaSymbol{\{}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{ev1-satisfies}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{theorem-eigenvector-1}\<%
\\
%
\>[2]\AgdaSymbol{;}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{ev2-satisfies}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{theorem-eigenvector-2}\<%
\\
%
\>[2]\AgdaSymbol{;}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{ev3-satisfies}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{theorem-eigenvector-3}\<%
\\
%
\>[2]\AgdaSymbol{\}}\<%
\end{code}

\section{Dimensionality and Independence}

To prove that these three eigenvectors form a basis for a 3-dimensional space, we must show they are linearly independent. We do this by calculating the determinant of the matrix formed by their components.

\begin{figure}[h]
\centering
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=1.3, z={(0.7,0.35)}]
  % Axes
  \draw[->, fdGray, thick] (0,0,0) -- (2.5,0,0) node[right] {$x$ ($e_1$)};
  \draw[->, fdGray, thick] (0,0,0) -- (0,2.5,0) node[above] {$y$ ($e_2$)};
  \draw[->, fdGray, thick] (0,0,0) -- (0,0,2.5) node[below left] {$z$ ($e_3$)};

  % Tetrahedron vertices in eigenspace
  \coordinate (v0) at (1.2,1.2,1.2);
  \coordinate (v1) at (1.2,-1,-1);
  \coordinate (v2) at (-1,1.2,-1);
  \coordinate (v3) at (-1,-1,1.2);

  % Edges
  \draw[thick, fdBlue] (v0) -- (v1);
  \draw[thick, fdBlue] (v0) -- (v2);
  \draw[thick, fdBlue] (v0) -- (v3);
  \draw[thick, fdBlue, dashed] (v1) -- (v2);
  \draw[thick, fdBlue] (v2) -- (v3);
  \draw[thick, fdBlue, dashed] (v3) -- (v1);

  % Vertices
  \fill[fdRed] (v0) circle (2.5pt) node[above right] {\small $v_0$};
  \fill[fdRed] (v1) circle (2.5pt) node[below right] {\small $v_1$};
  \fill[fdRed] (v2) circle (2.5pt) node[left] {\small $v_2$};
  \fill[fdRed] (v3) circle (2.5pt) node[below left] {\small $v_3$};

  % Annotation
  \node[right=2cm of v0, text width=5cm, align=left] {
    \textbf{Spectral Embedding}\\[0.3em]
    The 3-fold degenerate eigenvalue $\lambda=4$ spans a 3D eigenspace.\\[0.3em]
    \textit{Space is not a container—it is the symmetry of the graph.}
  };
\end{tikzpicture}
\caption{Emergence of 3D space. The three degenerate eigenvectors embed $K_4$ as a tetrahedron in $\mathbb{R}^3$.}
\label{fig:spectral-3d}
\end{figure}

\medskip
\noindent\textit{Summary:} The Laplacian spectrum is fully characterized: one eigenvalue 0 
(trivial mode) and three copies of eigenvalue 4 (spatial modes). This 3-fold degeneracy 
is the origin of three-dimensional space.

%% ============================================================
\chapter{The Emergence of Three Dimensions}
\label{chap:three-dimensions}
%% ============================================================

We have derived the spectrum: eigenvalue 4 with multiplicity 3. This is not a coincidence. 
The multiplicity of the principal eigenvalue defines the \emph{embedding dimension}—the 
number of independent directions in which the graph can be realized. Here, we see the 
number 3 emerging not as an axiom, but as a derived property of the $K_4$ structure.

\section{Eigenspace Dimension}

\begin{code}%
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{count-λ₄-eigenvectors}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaDatatype{ℕ}\<%
\\
%
\\[\AgdaEmptyExtraSkip]%

We formally constrain the dimension to be exactly three.

\begin{code}%
\>[0]\AgdaKeyword{data}\AgdaSpace{}%

\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaDatatype{ℕ}\AgdaSpace{}%

\AgdaKeyword{where}\<%

\AgdaInductiveConstructor{exactly-three}\<%
\\
\>[0]\<%

We prove that the dimension cannot be 2 or 4.

\begin{code}%
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{dimension-not-2}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{Impossible}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaFunction{EmbeddingDimension}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaDatatype{≡}}\AgdaSpace{}%

\\

\AgdaSymbol{()}\<%
\\
%

\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%

\begin{code}%
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{dimension-2-3-incompatible}\AgdaSpace{}%

\AgdaOperator{\AgdaInductiveConstructor{,}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{\AgdaUnderscore{})}\<%
\\
\>[0]\<%
\end{code}

These impossibility proofs are not approximations. The type $2 \equiv 3$ has no inhabitants---there is no term of this type in any consistent type theory. This is the formal content of ``3 is not 2.''

The linear independence of the eigenvectors is the key to this dimensionality.

\begin{code}%
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{theorem-all-three-required}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{det-eigenvectors}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaDatatype{≡}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{1ℤ}\<%
\\

\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{theorem-K4-linear-independence}\<%
\\
\>[0]\<%
\end{code}

\section{Spectral Embedding}

We can now map the vertices of the graph into this 3-dimensional spectral space. Each vertex $v$ is assigned a coordinate vector $(e_1(v), e_2(v), e_3(v))$.

\begin{code}%
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{SpectralPosition}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaPrimitive{Set}\<%
\\

We define the squared Euclidean distance in this spectral space.

\begin{code}%

Calculating the distances reveals the geometry. We find that $v_0$ is equidistant from $v_1, v_2, v_3$, and $v_1, v_2, v_3$ are equidistant from each other. The distance squared from $v_0$ is 6, while the distance between the others is 2. This suggests $v_0$ is at the apex of a tetrahedron, or perhaps the center of a star graph, depending on the projection.

Despite the apparent asymmetry in the spectral coordinates, the graph itself is vertex-transitive. We can define symmetries that map any vertex to any other while preserving the metric structure.

\begin{code}%

\section{Consilience of Dimension}

We have multiple ways to define the "dimension" of a graph. In $K_4$, all these definitions converge on the number 3. This consilience is a strong indicator that the 3-dimensionality of space is not an accident, but a necessary feature of the fundamental structure.

\begin{code}%

\section{Uniqueness of Three Dimensions}

Why three? The answer is not ``because 2 and 4 are wrong'' but because \emph{two independent derivations converge} on 3:
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{From vertex degree}: $d = V - 1 = 4 - 1 = 3$
\item \textbf{From eigenspace}: The multiplicity of $\lambda = 4$ is exactly 3
\end{itemize}
This structural convergence---algebraic (vertex count) and spectral (eigenvalue multiplicity)---makes 3 the unique dimension.

We verify the structural invariants of the graph.

\begin{code}%
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{theorem-lambda-from-k4}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{λ₄}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaDatatype{≡}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{mkℤ}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaNumber{4}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{zero}\<%
\\

\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{refl}\<%
\\
\>[0]\<%
\end{code}

The Euler characteristic $\chi = V - E + F$. For $K_4$ on a sphere (planar embedding), this is 2.

\textbf{Why the sphere?} $K_4$ is planar---it can be embedded in $\mathbb{R}^2$ (equivalently, on a sphere) 
without edge crossings. As the maximal complete planar graph 
(since $K_5$ requires crossings), $K_4$ determines the topology. For any planar embedding, the Euler formula gives $\chi = 2$.

The alternative (torus, $\chi = 0$) would require $K_4$ to be non-planar---but it IS planar. 
We don't choose $\chi = 2$; the planarity of $K_4$ forces it.

\begin{code}%
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{theorem-k4-euler-computed}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{K4-V}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaPrimitive{+}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{K4-V}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaDatatype{≡}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{K4-E}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaPrimitive{+}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{K4-chi}\<%
\\
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{theorem-k4-euler-computed}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{refl}\<%
\\
%
\\[\AgdaEmptyExtraSkip]%
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{theorem-chi-not-zero}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaFunction{¬}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaFunction{K4-chi}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaDatatype{≡}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaNumber{0}\AgdaSymbol{)}\<%
\\
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{theorem-chi-not-zero}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{()}\<%
\\
\>[0]\<%
\end{code}

\begin{code}%
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{theorem-deg-from-k4}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{K4-deg}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaDatatype{≡}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaNumber{3}\<%
\\
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{theorem-deg-from-k4}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{refl}\<%
\\
\>[0]\<%
\end{code}

\medskip
\noindent\textit{Summary:} The chain from $D_0$ to three dimensions is complete: 
$D_0 \to K_4 \to$ Laplacian $\to$ eigenvalue 4 with multiplicity 3 $\to$ 3D space.

%% ============================================================
\chapter{The Seven Constants}
\label{chap:physical-constants}
%% ============================================================

Having derived $K_4$, its spectrum, and three-dimensional space, we now turn to the 
quantitative predictions. The fundamental constants of physics—previously thought to be 
arbitrary inputs—emerge as structural invariants of $K_4$. These are not fitted; they 
are computed.

\section{The Derivation of Alpha}

The fine structure constant $\alpha \approx 1/137$ is one of the most famous numbers in physics. We find that the integer 137 emerges naturally from the combinatorics of the $K_4$ graph in 3 dimensions. The formula is $4^D \times 2 + 9$, where $D=3$.

\begin{code}%
\>[0]\AgdaKeyword{record}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaRecord{AlphaFormulaStructure}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaPrimitive{Set}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaKeyword{where}\<%
\\
\>[0][@{}l@{\AgdaIndent{0}}]%
\>[2]\AgdaKeyword{field}\<%
\\
\>[2][@{}l@{\AgdaIndent{0}}]%
\>[4]\AgdaField{lambda-value}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{λ₄}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaDatatype{≡}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{mkℤ}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaNumber{4}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{zero}\<%
\\
%
\>[4]\AgdaField{chi-value}%
\>[17]\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{K4-chi}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaDatatype{≡}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaNumber{2}\<%
\\
%

\>[17]\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{K4-deg}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaDatatype{≡}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaNumber{3}\<%
\\
%
\>[4]\AgdaField{main-term}%
\>[17]\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaFunction{K4-V}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaFunction{\textasciicircum{}}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{K4-deg}\AgdaSymbol{)}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaPrimitive{*}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{K4-chi}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaPrimitive{+}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaFunction{K4-deg}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaPrimitive{*}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{K4-deg}\AgdaSymbol{)}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaDatatype{≡}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaNumber{137}\<%
\\
%
\\[\AgdaEmptyExtraSkip]%
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{theorem-alpha-structure}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaRecord{AlphaFormulaStructure}\<%
\\
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{theorem-alpha-structure}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaKeyword{record}\<%
\\
\>[0][@{}l@{\AgdaIndent{0}}]%
\>[2]\AgdaSymbol{\{}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaField{lambda-value}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{theorem-lambda-from-k4}\<%
\\
%
\>[2]\AgdaSymbol{;}\AgdaSpace{}%

\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaInductiveConstructor{refl}\<%
\\
%

\\
%
\>[2]\AgdaSymbol{;}\AgdaSpace{}%

\\
%

\end{code}

If the dimension were 2 or 4, this value would be radically different.
The formula is $\lambda^D \times \chi + d^2$, where $\lambda$ = K4-V = 4, $\chi$ = 2, and $d$ = K4-deg = 3.
We vary D (the exponent) to show that only D = 3 yields 137.

\begin{code}%
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{alpha-if-d-equals-2}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaDatatype{ℕ}\<%
\\
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{alpha-if-d-equals-2}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaFunction{K4-V}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaFunction{\textasciicircum{}}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaNumber{2}\AgdaSymbol{)}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaPrimitive{*}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{K4-chi}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaPrimitive{+}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaFunction{K4-deg}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaPrimitive{*}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{K4-deg}\AgdaSymbol{)}\<%
\\
%
\\[\AgdaEmptyExtraSkip]%
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{alpha-if-d-equals-4}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaDatatype{ℕ}\<%
\\
\>[0]\AgdaFunction{alpha-if-d-equals-4}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaFunction{K4-V}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaFunction{\textasciicircum{}}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaNumber{4}\AgdaSymbol{)}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaPrimitive{*}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{K4-chi}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaPrimitive{+}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaFunction{K4-deg}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaPrimitive{*}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{K4-deg}\AgdaSymbol{)}\<%
\\
\>[0]\<%
\end{code}

We also check the ``kappa'' value, related to the coordination number.
Here $\kappa = 2 \times (d + 1)$ where $d$ is the embedding dimension.

\paragraph{Epistemic Status of the 5-Pillar Pattern.}
The 5-pillar verification structure (forced, consistency, exclusivity, robustness,
cross-validation) is a \emph{diagnostic pattern}, not a uniqueness theorem. It verifies:
\begin{enumerate}
\item \textbf{Forced}: The formula evaluates to the correct value given $K_4$ invariants.
  This is arithmetic, verified by \texttt{refl}.
\item \textbf{Consistency}: Multiple derivation paths (spectral, operadic, combinatorial)
  yield the same result. This is internal coherence, not ontological necessity.
\item \textbf{Exclusivity}: Changing parameters (e.g.\ using $d=2$ or $d=4$ instead of $d=3$)
  breaks the result. This shows sensitivity, not uniqueness of formula form.
\item \textbf{Robustness}: The formula's ingredients appear independently in other structures
  (dimension, Laplacian spectrum, Euler characteristic).
\item \textbf{Cross-validation}: Values used in the formula are not free parameters but are
  derived from the same $K_4$ structure.
\end{enumerate}
What the 5-pillar pattern does \emph{not} prove: that the \emph{form} of the formula
($\chi\lambda^d + d^2$) is forced. \S\ref{sec:alpha-formula-why} provides two independent
derivation paths (spectral and operadic) that converge on the decomposition $128 + 9$.
The exhaustion scan in \S\ref{sec:alpha-exhaustion} verifies no accidental collision
within a polynomial search space. What remains open is a first-principles proof
that the combination rule ``product $\times$ topology $+$ sum'' is the unique
physically meaningful functional form.

We summarize the derivation of Alpha, with the above epistemic caveat:

\section{Particle Mass Ratios}
\label{sec:mass-ratios-intro}

Beyond the fine structure constant, the geometry of $K_4$ also sheds light on the mass ratios of the fundamental leptons. We define the observed values (rounded to nearest integer) and compare them with values derived from the graph's combinatorial properties.

\medskip
\noindent\textit{Note: For the complete geometric derivation of lepton masses from $K_4$ invariants, see Chapter~\ref{chap:lepton-masses}.}

\begin{code}%

\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaDatatype{ℕ}\<%
\\

\AgdaSymbol{=}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaFunction{K4-deg}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaPrimitive{*}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{K4-deg}\AgdaSymbol{)}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaPrimitive{*}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaSymbol{(}\AgdaFunction{K4-E}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaOperator{\AgdaPrimitive{+}}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaFunction{F₂}\AgdaSymbol{)}\<%
\\
%
\\[\AgdaEmptyExtraSkip]%

%

\AgdaSymbol{:}\AgdaSpace{}%
\AgdaDatatype{ℕ}\<%
